caveats: a: The occupational source is 2023 and covers NAICS 485100, not the six-digit code.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares; higher-paid administrative and managerial jobs receive an approximate wage adjustment in the judgment.
a: Autonomous driving is treated conservatively because technical exposure is not the same as deployable labor substitution.
rho: The strongest implementation example is rural paratransit rather than urban rail or specialty transit.
rho: The estimate excludes pricing and demand effects and does not assume unattended autonomous vehicles.
e: No source directly identifies which frozen-band NAICS 485119 firms meet the lens.
e: GAO's contracting survey covers transit agencies and all modes rather than the private firms counted in this six-digit code.
e: The frozen n is an estimate derived from older receipts and size-class data.
s5: No industry-specific ownership-transfer series was found.
s5: The long contract terms documented for fixed-route bus contracts may not carry over to specialty urban transit modes.
s5: A transfer of an operating contract without equity control is not a qualifying event.
q: Contract evidence is from fixed-route bus service and may not represent rail, ferry, automated guideway, or mixed specialty services.
q: The range is a discounted five-year retention judgment, not an observed margin outcome.
d5: Observed ridership recovery partly reflects rebound from an abnormal pandemic trough.
d5: National aggregate demand is geographically concentrated and does not map cleanly to small private operators.
d5: Vehicle miles are an imperfect measure of constant-quality customer demand.
o: Automated guideway and closed-route applications could reduce operator labor faster than the base case.
o: The estimate separates continued need for an accountable firm from the number of employees required inside it.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring agency or institutional operating contracts, commonly paid per revenue hour or mile with possible fixed administrative fees or cost pass-throughs; some operators also receive passenger fares or subsidies.
lens.excluded_activities: Public authorities, captive municipal operations, financing vehicles, pure software vendors, construction-only contractors, and firms without transferable operating responsibility.
lens.included_activities: Privately owned operators of other urban transit modes that provide recurring, accountable operations, dispatch, maintenance, or integrated transit service to public agencies, institutions, or passengers and fall in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.

a.bridge: BLS measures the 4-digit Urban Transit Systems workforce rather than NAICS 485119 and reports occupation employment rather than task exposure. I mapped wage-bearing occupation groups to task-level AI exposure: high for dispatch, reservations, customer service, finance and administration; moderate for supervisors and managers; low for maintenance and the dominant vehicle-operator group. The broader 485100 mix may overstate rail-heavy 485119 exposure if the latter has more safety-critical operating labor, while omission of occupation detail within jobs may understate assistive opportunities.
caveats: The occupational source is 2023 and covers NAICS 485100, not the six-digit code.
Employment shares are not wage shares; higher-paid administrative and managerial jobs receive an approximate wage adjustment in the judgment.
Autonomous driving is treated conservatively because technical exposure is not the same as deployable labor substitution.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task inventory from several NAICS 485119 operators, including dispatcher handle time, call volumes, reporting hours, maintenance diagnostics, and non-driving operator duties, paired with observed AI pilot time savings.
a.rationale: In BLS's 485100 workforce, transportation and material-moving occupations are 72.63% of employment and passenger vehicle drivers alone are 60.73%, while office and administrative support is 8.72%, dispatchers are 2.97%, and business/financial roles are 2.22%. AI-based scheduling, exception triage, customer communications, compliance drafting, and back-office work expose a meaningful minority of wage-weighted tasks, but most driving and hands-on maintenance work is not currently substitutable in ordinary urban service.

d5.bridge: FTA and APTA measure national public-transit trips and vehicle service across all modes rather than constant-quality demand for private NAICS 485119 operators. I use the 2022-2025 ridership recovery and broadly stable 2014-2024 supplied service as directional evidence, normalize away fare and quality changes, and temper the trend because recovery from the pandemic is not steady-state growth. The all-mode series may overstate or understate specialty urban modes depending on their local commuter exposure.
caveats: Observed ridership recovery partly reflects rebound from an abnormal pandemic trough.
National aggregate demand is geographically concentrated and does not map cleanly to small private operators.
Vehicle miles are an imperfect measure of constant-quality customer demand.
d5.change_evidence: Mode-specific NTD monthly ridership, vehicle-revenue-mile, service-frequency, and contract-award data mapped specifically to private NAICS 485119 operations, with MPO five-year service plans and inflation-adjusted agency budgets.
d5.rationale: National ridership rose to 7.7 billion trips in 2024, up 7% year over year, and reached 85% of pre-pandemic levels in early 2025. FTA reports total capacity-equivalent vehicle revenue miles of 5.977 billion in 2024 versus 5.929 billion in 2014. Recovery, population growth, accessibility needs, and congestion support modest five-year real demand growth, offset by remote work, local funding stress, ride-hailing, and uneven mode recovery.

caveats: No source directly identifies which frozen-band NAICS 485119 firms meet the lens.
GAO's contracting survey covers transit agencies and all modes rather than the private firms counted in this six-digit code.
The frozen n is an estimate derived from older receipts and size-class data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm census of the ten frozen-band firms using NTD contractor records, SAM.gov awards, state corporate registries, ownership data, customer concentration, contract assignability, and normalized EBITDA verification.
e.rationale: The frozen firm count includes private businesses in the EBITDA band, but eligibility further requires recurring external-customer service, transferable operating responsibility, and control ownership. Contract operation is prevalent in transit, yet other urban transit also contains entities tied to specific assets, public-authority structures, or narrow concessions that may not be transferable as a standalone control acquisition.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 485119 ($107,237,280) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 485119 ($332,290,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[48]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83%); revenue/employee $229,641 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 485119); class <5: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $225,364 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $22,153 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,982,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,831 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,041,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,396 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 9 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,977,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $390,972 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $27,872,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,739,818 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,708,143 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,625,410 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 10

caveats: Automated guideway and closed-route applications could reduce operator labor faster than the base case.
The estimate separates continued need for an accountable firm from the number of employees required inside it.
o.change_evidence: Signed driverless-service authorizations, safety-case approvals, union agreements, insurance terms, and fully unattended revenue-service statistics for the specific modes operated by lens firms, plus agency procurement plans that replace operators with software-only supply.
o.rationale: Most year-five transit quantity should still require an accountable operator to employ and supervise staff, maintain vehicles, manage emergencies, meet accessibility and safety obligations, interface with agencies, and stand behind service levels. Software may eliminate portions of reservation, dispatch, reporting, and customer-service work, but does not remove the operating entity; full driverless substitution across ordinary public transit is unlikely within five years.

q.bridge: The National Academies survey measures third-party fixed-route bus contracts, not NAICS 485119 contracts. I translate its observed payment and renewal structures into retained-benefit economics: fee-per-hour or mile contracts allow savings to persist during a term, while cost pass-through clauses and competitive renewals share savings with the agency. Specialty-mode contracts may be less standardized, widening both directions of error.
caveats: Contract evidence is from fixed-route bus service and may not represent rail, ferry, automated guideway, or mixed specialty services.
The range is a discounted five-year retention judgment, not an observed margin outcome.
q.change_evidence: A clause-level review of current 485119 customer contracts covering escalation, gainsharing, cost audit, change-in-law, staffing minimums, service-level penalties, renewal, rebid, and change-of-control provisions, followed by customer procurement interviews.
q.rationale: Among surveyed fixed-route bus contracts, 69% used a fee per revenue hour or mile, 31% included cost pass-through, and 39% an administrative or overhead fee; 81% had maximum terms of five to ten years. These structures allow some implemented savings to remain with the operator during a contract, but cost disclosure, performance requirements, agency bargaining, rebids, and renewal pricing progressively transfer part of the benefit to customers.

rho.bridge: FTA documents one rural paratransit AI-dispatch deployment, while the lens is other urban transit operators. I use the demonstrated ability to manage more rides with the same number of dispatchers as evidence that exposed scheduling and communication tasks can be implemented, then discount for urban complexity, integrations, labor rules, procurement, cybersecurity, accessibility, and safety review. The rural setting likely overstates ease of implementation, but a small operator may have simpler legacy systems than a large agency.
caveats: The strongest implementation example is rural paratransit rather than urban rail or specialty transit.
The estimate excludes pricing and demand effects and does not assume unattended autonomous vehicles.
rho.change_evidence: Five-year before-and-after labor-hour and service-quality data from urban transit AI deployments, plus operator interviews on procurement cycles, collective-bargaining constraints, integration costs, and percentage of dispatch exceptions resolved without human intervention.
rho.rationale: AI dispatch has been deployed in transit and can absorb scheduling, no-show prediction, passenger notification, and exception-monitoring work. Five-year implementation should be material for digital workflows, but unionized operations, public procurement, fragmented data, service-quality obligations, and the human accountability required for vehicle operations keep implementation well below the full exposed opportunity.

caveats: No industry-specific ownership-transfer series was found.
The long contract terms documented for fixed-route bus contracts may not carry over to specialty urban transit modes.
A transfer of an operating contract without equity control is not a qualifying event.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, BizBuySell, and state-registry transaction histories for NAICS 485119 and adjacent contracted-transit operators, supplemented by owner-age and succession interviews for every eligible firm.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability near one quarter is a bounded judgment for small private operators: long-lived public contracts can support a sale, but customer concentration, change-of-control consent, bonding and safety qualifications reduce the set of executable buyers. Contract rebids are not counted as ownership transfers.

principal_driver: AI-enabled dispatch, scheduling, customer communication, and back-office throughput inside recurring operating contracts.
principal_weakness: A tiny, contract-concentrated target universe whose core vehicle-operation labor is difficult to substitute safely within five years.
